summary: Joel Spolsky celebrates 'duct tape programmers' like Jamie Zawinski — pragmatic developers who ship products by avoiding overengineered solutions. Drawing from Zawinski's interview in Coders at Work, Spolsky argues that overly complex technologies like C++ templates, multiple inheritance, COM, and multithreading doom projects because they exceed what the human brain can reliably manage. He champions the 'Worse is Better' philosophy: a 50% solution that ships beats a 99% solution stuck in the lab. However, Spolsky adds an important caveat — duct tape programming requires genuine talent to pull off, and not everyone can get away with skipping unit tests or cutting corners.
key_points: Pragmatic programmers who ship working software are more valuable than architecture astronauts who endlessly design elegant abstractions that never deliver
Technologies like C++ templates, multiple inheritance, COM, and multithreading are theoretically sound but practically too complex for reliable human cognition under real deadlines
Shipping is itself a feature — the most important one — and a good-enough solution that exists beats a perfect solution that doesn't
Many programmers are too afraid of looking stupid to push back against overengineered approaches, while duct tape programmers have the confidence to say 'stop it'
Duct tape programming is not a license for mediocrity — it requires exceptional talent to ship fast without creating disasters
money_quotes: A 50%-good solution that people actually have solves more problems and survives longer than a 99% solution that nobody has because it's in your lab where you're endlessly polishing the damn thing.
Shipping is a feature. A really important feature. Your product must have it.

At the end of the day, ship the fucking thing! It's great to rewrite your code and make it cleaner and by the third time it'll actually be pretty. But that's not the point—you're not here to write code; you're here to ship products.
It was decisions like not using C++ and not using threads that made us ship the product on time.
The duct-tape programmer is not afraid to say, 'multiple inheritance sucks. Stop it. Just stop.'

Duct tape programmers don't give a shit what you think about them. They stick to simple basic and easy to use tools and use the extra brainpower that these tools leave them to write more useful features for their customers.
Any kind of coding technique that's even slightly complicated is going to doom your project.
key_insight: The best programmers ship working products by choosing simple, reliable tools over impressive-sounding complex technologies, because shipping matters more than architectural elegance.
